summary: DHH reflects on how Ruby's welcoming community, particularly on ruby-talk, made his transition from PHP much easier. He highlights Matz's personal involvement in answering questions as a powerful motivating factor. DHH describes deliberately trying to replicate that friendliness in the Rails community through the #rubyonrails IRC channel, and shares testimonials from a newcomer who found the community exceptionally helpful and was able to build something functional despite being a total beginner.
key_points: Ruby's community stood out for its friendliness compared to PHP's scattered, fragmented communities
Having a language creator (Matz) personally engaged in answering questions is a powerful motivator for newcomers
Community friendliness is contagious — experiencing a welcoming community inspires you to create one
The #rubyonrails IRC channel was deliberately modeled after ruby-talk's welcoming culture, banning the RTFM attitude
A friendly community is one of a framework's biggest competitive assets
money_quotes: One thing that surprised me coming to Ruby was how extremely friendly everyone was.
One place to look for all the experts and they even bothered to answer my silly questions.
That's a pretty powerful motivating factor when the creator is still that involved and caring for his creation.
The best part of the friendly welcome is that it's contagious.
I doubt think the channel has ever heard the dread of RTFM!

I'm stunned that a framework which can create something as complex and professional as Basecamp can also allow a totally green newbie like me to make something cool and functional, without making me tear my hair out.
key_insight: A deliberately friendly and welcoming community is one of the most powerful assets a programming language or framework can have, because helpfulness is contagious and lowers the barrier for newcomers to build real things.
